hints.nominative: Nominative neuter is possible for 'infandum' but here it agrees with 'dolorem' (acc. masc.)
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'infando' — 'infandum' is accusative, modifying 'dolorem'
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'infandi' — 'infandum' is accusative here

steps.prompt: What declension is 'infandum' (as a 1st/2nd decl. adjective)?

hints.1st declension: 1st declension covers the feminine forms (-a) — the -um ending is 2nd declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension adjectives have genitive in -is — 'infandum' follows -us, -a, -um pattern
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'infandum' is a 2nd declension adjective form

hints.nominative: While 'regina' looks nominative, Aeneas is addressing the queen directly — it is vocative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'regina' as well in 1st declension, but context shows direct address (vocative)
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'reginae' — 'regina' here is vocative, direct address

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'reginae' ends in -ae (1st declension)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'reginae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'reginae' ends in -ae

hints.to judge: That would be 'iudicare' — 'iubes' comes from 'iubere' (to order)
hints.to help: That would be 'iuvare' — 'iubes' comes from 'iubere' (to order)
hints.to please: That would be 'placere' — 'iubes' comes from 'iubere' (to order)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'iubeas' — 'iubes' with -e- is present indicative (2nd conj.)
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'iubebis' — 'iubes' is present indicative
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'iussisti' — 'iubes' is present indicative

hints.iudico, iudicare, iudicavi, iudicatum: These are the parts of 'iudicare' (to judge)
hints.iungo, iungere, iunxi, iunctum: These are the parts of 'iungere' (to join)
hints.iuvo, iuvare, iuvi, iutum: These are the parts of 'iuvare' (to help)

hints.present indicative: 'renovare' ending in -are is the infinitive, not a finite verb form
hints.future infinitive: Future infinitive would be 'renovaturum esse' — 'renovare' is present infinitive
hints.perfect infinitive: Perfect infinitive would be 'renovavisse' — 'renovare' is present infinitive

hints.removeo, removere, removi, remotum: These are the parts of 'removere' (to remove)
hints.reddo, reddere, reddidi, redditum: These are the parts of 'reddere' (to return/give back)
hints.relinquo, relinquere, reliqui, relictum: These are the parts of 'relinquere' (to leave behind)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'dolor' — the -em ending marks the accusative singular
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'dolore' — 'dolorem' with -em is accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'dolori' — 'dolorem' with -em is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'doloris' ends in -is (3rd declension)
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'doloris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'doloris' ends in -is

translation: Unspeakable grief, O queen, you bid me renew
